title: Berliner jam doughnuts
subtitle: German deep-fried yeast doughnuts filled with raspberry jam
excerpt: Round, deep-fried yeast doughnuts without a hole, filled with raspberry jam through a piped hole in the side and rolled in caster sugar while still warm. The dough is enriched with egg and butter for a tender, pillowy interior that stays soft for hours. Berliners are the reference for the filled doughnut: round, light, and barely sweet on their own so the filling is the primary flavour.
sourceNotes: The Berliner (also called Pfannkuchen in Berlin itself, and krapfen in southern Germany and Austria) is documented in German and central European baking literature from the seventeenth century as a carnival and festival food. Henriette Davidis, Praktisches Kochbuch (1847, nineteenth-century German domestic cookery standard, digitised editions available), describes filled yeast doughnuts as a standard German confectionery item. The round format, jam filling, and sugar rolling are described in German and Austrian domestic guides published before 1928.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day of frying. The proved, uncooked doughnuts can be refrigerated for up to 12 hours; bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before frying.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried in vegetable oil at 175°C. A thermometer is essential: too hot and the outside browns before the centre cooks through; too cool and the doughnuts absorb oil and are heavy.

recipeTools.notes: With dough hook; useful for the enriched dough.
Deep enough for the doughnuts to float in at least 6 cm of oil.
For piping jam into the baked doughnuts.

glossaryTerms.definition: Keeping the frying oil at a steady 175°C throughout the frying process. Adding doughnuts drops the temperature; if the oil drops below 165°C the doughnuts absorb fat and become greasy. If it rises above 185°C they brown before cooking through. Fry in small batches of 3 to 4, checking the temperature between each batch and adjusting the heat. A sugar thermometer is essential; estimating by eye is unreliable. Allow the oil to recover to 175°C before adding each batch.

content.text: The most important variable is oil temperature.

throughout the frying is the difference between a light, evenly browned doughnut and a greasy, pale one. Small batches and a thermometer are non-negotiable. Roll in caster sugar while the doughnuts are still warm so the sugar adheres.

items.prepNote: smooth; sieve if it has seeds

content.text: Mix the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar in a bowl. Add the warm milk, eggs, and vanilla and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Work in the soft butter and continue kneading for 4 minutes. Cover and leave to rise for 60 minutes until doubled.

Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces (about 80 g each). Shape each into a smooth, tight ball by pinching the base. Place on lined trays, cover with oiled cling film, and prove for 45 minutes until puffed and light.

Heat the oil in a deep saucepan to 175°C. Lower doughnuts in, 3 to 4 at a time, and fry for 2 to 3 minutes per side until evenly deep golden. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain briefly on paper, then roll in caster sugar while still warm. When cool enough to handle, push a piping bag nozzle into the side of each doughnut and pipe in about 2 tablespoons of jam.
